speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] March 29th, 1996, interview with Fred Roer. Would you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, we would eat-- each other's back. We would get the liquid from there. The train ride was about six to eight days. When we came into the next camp, which was Gross-Rosen, half of the people in the, in the train were dead. We stored the deads on one side of the coal wagon. We covered ourselves with the dead at night. And that's the first time on that trip-- it's the first time I saw somebody eating a human being, or meat of a human being. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Because we, we only got three slices of bread when we get on it. We were, we were exposed to the cold. Everybody was more than hungry from marching, and so forth, and so on. That's the first time I saw that. In Gross-Rosen we were put into the camp. The camp itself was naturally overcrowded with the additional people coming from all-- from Auschwitz and all this part of Poland. We were laying like sardines in rows in the barracks. If you had to go to the bathroom at night, all you could do is step on people to get out of the door. And this is how Gross-Rosen was. At that time, it was more of a transit camp for, for let people go out again. And a lot of people were killed there and died there because from exhaustion and overexposure to all the, to all the situations we got into. From Gross-Rosen I was sent with 500 other inmates to Regensburg. In Regensburg we were located into a ballroom. On the floor was straw. And there were two toilets and two wash basins for 500 people. And the work we did there was working on railroad tracks, which at the moment, they were an operation would be bombed against by the, by the Allies, mostly Americans. We would be laying out in the open fields. We were not allowed to go into a bunker, and the bombs would fall around us. But luckily nobody really got hurt from it. Every time we were able to have a track moving again, the bombs would come and the track would be bombed out again. Again there, I'm speaking a lot of food, because that was the only idea you had and the only thing you were thinking of, to get additional food. There again, I ran into a certain kind of luck, because the food we got in the camp was not-- was even less than before in any camp. What happened there was there was a train, a military train with ammunition in the, in the switchyard there. There was a train with potatoes there. And there was a train with sugar there, which was supposed to go to the front. Well, when the bombs come, the bombs hit all-- hit the ammunition train. The ammunition train started burning and the potatoes were-- went into baked potatoes. The sugar was burnt sugar. And the train, the ammunition train, kept on exploding and shooting bullets for two or three days. And the guards naturally were afraid to go even close to it. We would crawl underneath the wagons and would get baked potatoes and burned sugar to supplement our food. And this went on for every time the bombs came, there was something there to eat or to explode or something for us to, to substitute our food with.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how I, how I survived Regensburg. The camp itself, like I said, was just a ballroom. The straw was so full of lice I think it would move an inch or two every night, every day when we were out on work.

speaker: interviewee
text: The washing facilities, or the toilets, would be always plugged up. And everything would just run out of the washrooms into the place where you slept. And but we were so dirty and so down by the end of the war that we really didn't care anymore. This was, in short, more or less what happened in Regensburg. Like I said, we laid in the open fields when the bombs were coming. The bombers were coming, and we would be happy to see them because the closer they came, the better it was for us. Then all of a sudden we could hear the explosion of, of artillery fire. And we found out that the Americans were coming on that way. And then the end of April, all of a sudden we were again put into columns to march. We marched towards Mauthausen. And we marched for about 10 days in the foothills up the Alps, still with snow. Most of us didn't have any shoes left because the Germans didn't give us anything, just the wooden planks on the bottom, half of it worn off. We would march in that through the snow. And a lot of people, up to 500. After 10 days when we were liberated, we were with about 180 left. The other ones were all fallen by the wayside, as we would say. We came to a little marketplace. All of a sudden-- well, the night before the last night, I was so exhausted that in the morning, was hiding in the store in the barn. Well, all of a sudden, the Germans came with the, with the, with the German police dogs and sniffed out the people what were hiding. And the, the commander from the guard, the camp leader said, well, when we're out of town, you will be shot, anybody who hides.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during that day, what I did, I went every time he was-- it was on the opposite side of the column marching. So all day long, I was switching from one side to the other. And luckily we stopped early. Around 2 o'clock he, he came to a marketplace. When he saw me there, he said, oh, no, I forgot to get-- I forgot about you. Well, tomorrow is another day. You will be killed tomorrow, you know? Like, well, throw you away tomorrow. What's the different?

speaker: interviewee
text: But then all of a sudden we notice the guards were disappearing. We were sitting in the marketplace when they were disappearing. All of a sudden, we were all by ourselves. Then the police of that little-- of that town there, Lauffen, the police of Lauffen came and, and didn't know what to do with us. So in the neighborhood there's a city, a little town called Liebenau. And in Liebenau there was a prison for women, German women-- prostitutes, antisocial, anti-political. They were there and were, were working on German uniforms. So they, they cleared one barrack of that prison and put us in there to hold us. Now, this, this guy who ran the prison there, he really felt sorry for us. He didn't know what to do. So he came and gave us bread, double rations bread. And so we there for two nights, two days. Then all of a sudden on Friday night-- by that time the, the American army didn't fight at night anymore, especially-- that prison was inside a forest. They didn't take any chances anymore because the war was won. There was no reason to expose the soldier to a sniper or anything. So when the dusk started, they stopped advancing. Well, there were some people in that-- in this army who heard from, from people, from civilians there that there in the-- in this prison, there are some prisoners, some Jews being held from concentration camps. So about 40, 50 soldiers volunteered, mostly Jewish guys, to take a truck and go there and see that the Nazis don't come back during the night and kill us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of a sudden Friday evening, all of a sudden a truck of soldiers comes there, American soldiers. Naturally, we didn't know what to do. When they saw us, the guys started crying. You didn't know what to say. One walks into the room where we was in. And one of the prisoners says, [NON-ENGLISH], where are you, to the soldier. And he looks him. Who are you? How do you know me? Don't you know who I am? I'm your cousin. You went five years ago to America. Don't you recognize me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't recognize him, each other because we were just skeletons. And the soldier was, was his cousin who went from Poland to America. Well, them guys right away took the truck and went out again and went to a POW camp where there were Red Cross packages, 21, 22 pound packages. Loaded that truck with packages and came and gave us the food, which was the worst thing they could have done because we all were skeletons. We weren't used to any food, not spam, and not sardines, and for sure not butter and stuff like that. Well, a lot of us got sick from it, and some of them even, I would say, died on it. But then two days later, the medics came, the American medics. Well, the first thing they did, they took everything away, all the food. Then they started shearing us up again and rubbing us up with, with disinfections because we were full of lice and dirty. The clothes we had in one pile and burned it all. Then they gave us-- all they had was underwear, German underwear. That's all they had to give us. So we all had German underwear on running through the women camp. We could have cared less. We all were skeletons. I, I weighed 90 pounds. And I was one of the heaviest because I have a heavy bone structure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then the medics started us in with, with cereals. The way we looked, there was one driver, a medic, a big guy, and he had a bottle of whiskey. He said that bottle of whiskey, I saved that for the end of the war. I took it all from Normandy to here, he said, but you guys can have it. I wouldn't be able to drink it. Well, three of us emptied the neck of the bottle and we all three were drunk. So there you can see in which, which-- how we looked and what we were. Well, they made out of the barracks there they made right away like hospitals and started feeding us the, the cereals and advanced there. Well, we all were sick. Each one had something else-- typhus, swollen legs, ulcers, frostbite. Everything. You name it, we had it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself, after about a week, fell into a coma for about 10 days with typhus. And my legs were double the size full with water and ulcers. My feet were frostbitten.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after, after that, the Americans start giving us more food and better food. And they put some person in front of-- in charge of it to, to feed us, to have the kitchen [? under ?]. When they would roll in the barracks was about I would say about 30, 40 feet long, more than that maybe. They rolled in a wagon with food.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time they came to the middle of the room, all the food was gone, because we were still like animals. Whenever we saw food, we grabbed it and ate it. In fact, I doubled my weight in about two months. And so you get a-- which was unnatural. It wasn't healthy at all. But that is how, how we were liberated there. Then from there I went-- later on I went back to the city, this Kerpen, this little town we used to live. Because if my brother or my mother would be alive, that would be the place we would meet again. When I got there, these friends of ours right away said, you're not going to go back any place. You're going to stay here and live with us, which I did. It was really a family what never was Nazis. Was strictly Catholics and never, never even thought of it. In fact, their daughter was the person what was the night before we were evacuated from Cologne came to our house and to see us and give us stuff even when she knew we had to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with them. It was a mistake I made because if I had stayed in the American zone where I was liberated, I would have come to America a lot earlier. Being in the British zone, I was one of the first one who was able to come. But it took till '49 before I was able to come to the States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had uncles in LA. I had four different-- five different affidavits, but they took the one in LA. So when I came to New York, they send me to LA because I want the affidavit, the Jewish organization. This was the worst experience I ever had. They just put us on the train. They didn't let us call anybody. I knew I had relatives in New York, which would have come right away. And they put me on train, no you can't call anybody. You get on a train to LA. And they put us on the train in New York, and we went to Chicago. And in Chicago, we had to travel from one station to the other. And the traveler's aid was the one who held that we didn't see any Jewish organization anymore. In fact, my uncle sent money to New York so I would have some money to be on the train. Never got it. Luckily I had 20 $1 bills, which my aunt had put into letters to Germany only I had saved. So I had some money because we had to buy food and everything to-- we didn't have anything on the train. The train ride, at that time, from New York to LA took four days. In Chicago we went shopping near the railway station. We never went far, far away from it to make sure that we could see the station because we couldn't speak English. We didn't-- two words we knew. When we bought something, we showed what we wanted. We went to the little stores by the, by the train station where everything was probably was double the price. And before we left in Germany, they gave us some instruction what to do, what America expect. So we went to the counter in the railway station in Chicago and ordered a Coke. And we tipped the girl because they told us in America when you buy food, you tip. We didn't have any money and we were tipping. So there you can see how, how greenhorns we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Seattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Seattle in, in January of 1950. I came here because of complications. I tried to get into the plumber's union and then never was accepted because the GIs were coming back. And the fathers had their sons go in first because the union, at that time, were very strong and very close. I worked in Santa Rosa for a Jewish outfit. And he wanted to get me in. He couldn't. I worked in Petaluma for a non-Jew, and he couldn't swing it. I worked in, in San Francisco and I couldn't do it. Then my uncle, he had a friend who was acquainted with a plumber here in Seattle. And he said, send him up to Seattle, I get him in. When I worked here for eight weeks at the plumbing, that plumber tried everything possible to get me in. He went so far that the union told him if he works here tomorrow, we're going to close your shop. And he had 40 employees there, so he didn't have any choice. After that, I had a friend, a German boy. He was-- he came here from Shanghai. When I met him, we became friends. And he was working in a drapery wholesale outfit, and that is where-- I got a job there opening cases. And I stayed with this outfit for 32 years until we're closed up here in Seattle. And by the end, I was running the warehouse. We had 15 people there working for me on. So I worked my way up, more or less. Well, I worked my way up to assistant warehouse manager within three years. And then I was-- later on, I managed the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In conclusion, is there a message that you have, based on your life experience, that you'd like to pass on to your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is, under no circumstances ever hate anybody. Treat every human being like you want to be treated as a human being. And fight any discrimination in any way, in any which way, any way you can think of. And never be afraid to speak up. Like the saying now is, never again. But you have to, you have to work for it. If you keep quiet, that 'never again' can happen very easy. And don't think in America it cannot happen. It can happen here too. Look at Mr. Buchanan right now, and he is not the only one. And he is as scary as Hitler was because he gives the same message to the people, he tells them the same story, America over all close up everything. And in Germany, it was Germany over all, and still is. The anti-Semitism in Germany as I read and as I hear is still very, very strong. And let's not kid ourselves, all this thing can happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the photograph I was talking about on the Kristallnacht, which was hanging on the wall in my Grandma's home. As you can see, the back of the [? axe ?] is still showing. This is a little picture. This is of my great-grandparents, the parents of my grandfather. Their name was Leiser, L-E-I-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full name, and if you know the approximate date.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the date. This is a picture of my dad before he was-- I would say either in 1926 or '27 was taken there. I don't know the occasion for what it was taken. This is a picture taken of my family before we were deported. When all the family was already-- had already immigrated, we send it to them at that time. My mother was killed when Lodz was liquidated, and she was sent to Auschwitz. My brother died in March, the 10th of March, 1945, just couple of months before the Liberation in Dora-Mittelbau, which is an outer camp, I think, of Sachsenhausen. This is a picture of my sister-in-law, as I mentioned before, which was not allowed to go with us. The Nazis just didn't allowed her to come with her husband at the time because they said they weren't married long enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Edith-- well, Frank, F-R-A-N-K, before she was married to my brother Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Jewish too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she-- what ended up happening to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was sent from this little town Kerpen or Buir, where she lived last, to a camp. And I don't have any idea what happened to the whole transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said before, in this little town, there were about 30 families. And 100 people were-- 80-- or 20 about were able to immigrate. And of the 80 left, I'm the only survivor of the town. A lot of people were killed. Nobody know where they were sent to. This is a picture of the soccer team before the war. I'm the third from the left. This is a card which I sent from Posen from the camp to my sister-in-law. As I mentioned before, we were allowed little time to send cards. This is a telegram which was sent between my uncles in Columbia from one city to the other to show that I was alive, the first message they got that I had survived the camp. This is our wedding picture on February 24th, 1952 at Congregation Ezra Bessaroth, a Sephardic synagogue in Seattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Sarah, was Sarah Ismael. This picture was taken in 1990 on my 70th birthday, my wife and myself. This is a picture of our oldest son Carl with his wife Jackie. She is also from Seattle. Her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE]. This are my son childrens, Elana and Marcus. This is my son Greg, his wife Dorothy, their children Sarah, David, and Uri. Picture's about five years old. This is my daughter Liz, Elizabeth, with her husband Shlomo and their children Yitzchok, Yaakov, and Reina. Also about-- the picture is also about five years old. These are the two youngest children of Elizabeth and Shlomo. This Mattisyahu and Avraham. Mattisyahu doesn't have his hair cut yet. It's shortly before his fifth birthday.
